stem:
A 28-year-old man is brought to a Hunter New England LHD emergency department by police at 2330 after smashing windows at a service station. He paces in cubicle 4, shouts intermittently, refuses to sit, and has clenched fists. He has no known medical history per the brief paramedic handover, no medication list, and no IV access. He answers questions with hostility but is oriented to place and person and is not floridly hallucinating.

The triage nurse offers a quiet area and a drink of water; he kicks the chair away. Security are present at the door. Vital signs from a brief visual check: appears flushed and tachypnoeic, no pallor. He weighs approximately 80 kg. The on-call senior emergency physician asks for your plan for pharmacological management if verbal de-escalation continues to fail.

data_table.Examination: Pacing, clenched fists, raised voice, no obvious head injury, no track marks visible, no medical alert bracelet.
data_table.Investigations: None yet obtainable; refusing observations and venepuncture.

lead_in: After a further attempt at verbal de-escalation, which is the single most appropriate first-line agent for involuntary pharmacological sedation in this scenario?

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg orally
options.rationale: Oral route requires cooperation which is absent; onset 30-60 min is too slow for the immediate risk. Would be correct in a cooperative patient with mild agitation accepting oral therapy (Source: NSW Health Management of patients with Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in Emergency Departments).
source_refs: NSW Health PD2015_007 Management of Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg intramuscularly
options.rationale: Per NSW Health acute severe behavioural disturbance pathway, IM olanzapine 5-10 mg is a first-line monotherapy option when verbal de-escalation fails and IV access is not feasible; onset 15-30 min, broader margin of safety than droperidol in unknown drug or alcohol exposure, and avoids the airway concerns of high-dose benzodiazepine co-administration. NSW guidance explicitly warns against combining IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines within 1 hour due to cardiorespiratory depression.

options.text: Droperidol 10 mg intramuscularly combined with midazolam 5 mg intramuscularly
options.rationale: Combination IM sedation increases over-sedation, hypoxia and aspiration risk without clear benefit over droperidol monotherapy in this presentation. Would be correct only in extreme disturbance per a senior-led local protocol with continuous monitoring (Source: eTG Psychotropic; Calver 2015 DORM-II trial).

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly without a baseline ECG
options.rationale: Haloperidol is reasonable but NSW pathways prefer droperidol or olanzapine when an ECG is unavailable, due to a relatively higher QT prolongation risk and the lack of monotherapy outcome data in undifferentiated ED agitation. Would be correct if olanzapine and droperidol are unavailable and an ECG can be obtained promptly (Source: AMH; eTG).

options.text: Ketamine 4 mg/kg intramuscularly
options.rationale: IM ketamine is reserved for failure of first-line agents under senior emergency physician direction with full resuscitation readiness, not as initial pharmacological sedation. Would be correct after failure of olanzapine or droperidol in extreme cases with senior approval (Source: ACEM position statement).
source_refs: ACEM Acute behavioural disturbance position statement

explanation.summary: NSW Health PD2015_007 frames management of acute severe behavioural disturbance as a graded de-escalation pathway: environment, verbal de-escalation, oral therapy if accepted, then IM sedation if involuntary. IM olanzapine 5-10 mg or droperidol 10 mg are first-line monotherapies; combinations carry disproportionate over-sedation risk and are not recommended as initial choice. Vital signs, SpO2 and continuous observation are required post-sedation.
key_points: Verbal de-escalation precedes pharmacology where any window exists
First-line IM monotherapy in NSW: olanzapine 5-10 mg or droperidol 10 mg
Do not combine IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines within 1 hour
ECG and ongoing observation required post-sedation
Document the legal framework: common-law emergency vs Mental Health Act provisions
explanation.pearls: If alcohol or unknown sedative on board, olanzapine is generally preferred over benzodiazepine-based regimens because of additive respiratory depression risk with benzodiazepines.

sources.label: NSW Health PD2015_007 Management of Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance in EDs

subtopic_detail: First-line IM sedation choice for severe agitation without IV access

stem:
A 41-year-old woman is brought to a Central Coast LHD emergency department by her sister, who reports two weeks of escalating belief that the neighbours are pumping gas through the wall vents. She has barricaded her bedroom door, has not slept for three nights, and tonight threatened her sister with a kitchen knife before being talked down. She has no past psychiatric admission, no substance use history, and takes no medications.

On arrival she is calm but adamant she will leave as soon as she has used the toilet because 'the gas will be worse here'. She refuses voluntary admission, refuses bloods and refuses any medication. There is no immediate physical threat, but the ED registrar judges her at significant risk of harm to herself or others if she leaves now. The on-call psychiatry registrar is 40 minutes away.

data_table.Examination: Alert, oriented, thought disorder absent, persecutory delusion fixed, no command hallucinations elicited, no suicidal ideation, no current aggression.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate legal mechanism to detain her in the emergency department under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007?

options.text: Detain under a Schedule 1 Form completed by the ED medical officer as a mentally ill or mentally disordered person
options.rationale: Under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007 Schedule 1, a medical practitioner (including the ED medical officer) may certify that a person appears to be a mentally ill or mentally disordered person, authorising detention at a declared mental health facility for examination. The threshold is met here: belief that may be a delusion plus a recent serious risk-related act and ongoing risk if she leaves.
source_refs: NSW Mental Health Act 2007 s19, Sch 1
NSW Health Mental Health for Emergency Departments Reference Guide

options.text: Wait for the psychiatry registrar to attend and authorise detention
options.rationale: An ED medical officer is authorised to complete a Schedule 1; waiting 40 minutes while she is actively trying to leave is not lawful detention and is clinically unsafe. Would be appropriate only if she were already agreeing to remain (Source: NSW MHA 2007).
source_refs: NSW Mental Health Act 2007 s19, Sch 1

options.text: Apply common-law restraint without a Schedule 1 because the situation is an emergency
options.rationale: Common-law emergency restraint applies only to immediately life-threatening situations where a statutory pathway cannot be used; the Mental Health Act pathway is available and must be used in preference. Would be correct for a brief restraint while the Schedule 1 is being completed but not as the substantive authority (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).
source_refs: NSW Health PD2012_035 Aggression, Seclusion & Restraint

options.text: Request police invoke section 22 of the Mental Health Act 2007 to detain her
options.rationale: Section 22 applies only when police encounter a person in a public place; once in the ED, the medical pathway under Schedule 1 is the correct mechanism. Would be correct if she were in the carpark refusing to come inside (Source: NSW MHA 2007 s22).

options.text: Apply for a Community Treatment Order to compel her to remain
options.rationale: A CTO is made by the Mental Health Review Tribunal for community follow-up; it has no role in acute ED detention. Would be considered downstream after assessment and treatment in hospital (Source: NSW Mental Health Act 2007 part 3).
source_refs: NSW Mental Health Act 2007 Community Treatment Orders

explanation.summary: In NSW, the immediate legal authority to detain a patient in ED for psychiatric assessment is Schedule 1 of the Mental Health Act 2007, completed by a medical practitioner. The threshold is appearing to be a 'mentally ill person' (mental illness causing serious risk of harm) or 'mentally disordered person' (irrational behaviour creating serious risk in the last 24 hours). Once detained, an examination by an authorised medical officer must occur within 12 hours of arrival at a declared facility, and a second examination by a psychiatrist within a further 24 hours.
key_points: Schedule 1 is completed by a medical practitioner in any setting
Mentally ill: mental illness causing serious risk of harm
Mentally disordered: irrational behaviour creating serious risk in last 24 h
Police s22 applies in public place; not after ED arrival
CTOs are for community treatment, not acute detention
explanation.pearls: Document the specific risks, the alternative interventions considered, and the legal threshold met; this is reviewed by the MHRT.

sources.label: NSW Health Mental Health for ED Reference Guide

subtopic_detail: Schedule 1 detention under NSW Mental Health Act 2007

stem:
A 36-year-old man with chronic schizophrenia attends a regional NSW emergency department in acute paranoid agitation after running out of his depot two weeks ago. He weighs 95 kg. He accepts oral lorazepam 2 mg but remains threatening 30 minutes later. The team plans IM sedation. His regular outpatient ECG from last month shows sinus rhythm at 78 bpm with a QTc of 470 ms (Bazett).

He takes no other QT-prolonging medication. He has a known family history of sudden cardiac death in his father at age 48 of unknown cause. His electrolytes from a clinic visit a week ago: K 4.0 mmol/L, Mg 0.85 mmol/L (both within reference). He is now refusing further oral therapy and is pacing.

data_table.Examination: Agitated, no acute medical distress, alert, no focal neurology, BP 145/88, HR 110, SpO2 98% room air.
data_table.Investigations: ECG QTc 470 ms one month ago; current ECG not obtainable due to non-cooperation.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next pharmacological step?

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg
options.rationale: Droperidol prolongs the QT and would not be the safest single choice given a baseline QTc of 470 ms (already at the upper limit) and an unexplained family history of sudden cardiac death. Would be correct if QTc were under 450 ms and there were no SCD family history (Source: eTG; AMH).

options.text: IM olanzapine 10 mg
options.rationale: Olanzapine has the most favourable QT profile of the IM antipsychotic options used in Australian ED practice and is preferred when a baseline QTc is borderline or there is a family history of unexplained SCD. NSW Health guidance explicitly avoids combining IM olanzapine with parenteral benzodiazepines; the patient has already received oral lorazepam, so a delay of at least 1 hour applies if further benzodiazepine is later considered.

options.text: IM haloperidol 5 mg plus IM promethazine 25 mg
options.rationale: Haloperidol carries the same QT concern as droperidol; addition of promethazine sedates but does not address the QT risk. Would be reasonable in a low-QT-risk patient where olanzapine is unavailable (Source: AMH).

options.text: IM midazolam 10 mg
options.rationale: Benzodiazepine monotherapy at 10 mg IM risks respiratory depression in an antipsychotic-naive moment; behaviour driven by acute psychosis is better addressed with an antipsychotic. Would be correct in alcohol or stimulant-driven agitation without psychosis (Source: eTG).

options.text: IM ziprasidone 20 mg
options.rationale: Ziprasidone carries the highest QT prolongation among atypical antipsychotics and is not first-line in Australia for this indication; also not stocked in most NSW EDs. Would be correct if available and QT not a concern (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Choice of parenteral sedation in acute behavioural disturbance is shaped by QT risk, drug-on-drug interactions, and the route already used. With a borderline QTc (470 ms) and family history of unexplained SCD, droperidol and haloperidol are less attractive; olanzapine is the safer atypical option. Recent oral benzodiazepine pushes the timing of any further parenteral benzodiazepine outside the 1-hour window of IM olanzapine.
key_points: QTc >=450 ms in men or >=470 ms in women is borderline; >=500 ms is high-risk
Olanzapine has the lowest QT impact of common IM agents
Droperidol and haloperidol both carry QT risk; check baseline ECG when possible
Avoid IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour
Family history of unexplained early SCD lowers the threshold to avoid QT prolongers
explanation.pearls: Document the QT reasoning explicitly in the medical record; this is a common site of medico-legal scrutiny when ED sedation is followed by an adverse event.

subtopic_detail: Sedation choice when QTc is borderline and SCD family history is positive

stem: An 84 kg man receives IM olanzapine 10 mg in the resus bay of a Hunter New England LHD ED after pharmacological sedation became necessary for severe agitation. He was placed in supine four-point physical restraint by trained staff for the procedure. He is now drowsy but rousable. The bedside nurse asks what specific monitoring and reassessment cadence is required in the first hour.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy, eyes open to voice, GCS 13, no obvious trauma.
data_table.Investigations: Initial vitals post-sedation: HR 96, BP 132/78, RR 14, SpO2 95% on room air, temp 36.8.

lead_in: Which monitoring and re-evaluation plan most closely matches NSW Health guidance for the first hour after IM sedation in a restrained patient?

options.text: Continuous SpO2 and cardiac monitoring; HR, BP, RR, SpO2 and sedation score every 10 minutes for at least 1 hour; restraint reviewed and released as soon as clinically safe; documented review of restraint by a senior clinician within 4 hours
options.rationale: NSW Health PD2012_035 (Aggression, Seclusion and Restraint) and the ED sedation pathway specify continuous cardiac and SpO2 monitoring with at least 10-minute observations of HR, BP, RR, SpO2 and sedation score after IM sedation. Physical restraint must be released as soon as clinically safe, with a senior review and full documentation including the legal authority, indication, and alternatives considered.
source_refs: NSW Health PD2012_035 Aggression, Seclusion and Restraint

options.text: Hourly vitals for 4 hours; restraint maintained for at least 4 hours regardless of sedation level
options.rationale: Hourly intervals miss early respiratory depression after IM sedation; restraint duration is not pre-set and must be released when no longer clinically required. Would not match any current Australian ED guidance (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Vitals every 30 minutes for 2 hours; no requirement for continuous SpO2 if initial saturation is at least 94%
options.rationale: Continuous SpO2 monitoring is required for at least the first hour after IM sedation due to delayed-onset respiratory depression. Would be appropriate only after the initial close-observation period (Source: NSW Health PD2015_007).

options.text: ECG every 15 minutes for the first hour; vitals every 30 minutes; restraint maintained until oral medication is accepted
options.rationale: Serial ECGs are not routinely required; continuous cardiac monitoring is sufficient. Restraint duration is tied to clinical safety, not the patient's willingness to accept oral medication (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Vitals every 5 minutes for the first hour; full restraint maintained throughout; no documentation required until the patient is medically cleared
options.rationale: 5-minute observations are not required and burden staff without evidence of benefit beyond 10-minute monitoring; restraint review and documentation are mandatory in real time, not deferred. Would not meet NSW documentation standards (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

explanation.summary: NSW Health requires continuous cardiac and oximetry monitoring with at least 10-minute vitals and sedation-score observation for the first hour after IM sedation, with restraint released as soon as clinically safe. Senior clinician review and contemporaneous documentation of restraint use are required.
key_points: Continuous SpO2 + cardiac monitoring for first hour after IM sedation
HR, BP, RR, SpO2 and sedation score at least every 10 minutes
Restraint released as soon as clinically safe
Senior review and documentation of restraint within hours, not days
Re-orient and debrief patient post-event
explanation.pearls: Supine restraint risks positional asphyxia; never restrain prone; chest must be free for full respiratory excursion.

sources.label: NSW Health PD2012_035 Aggression, Seclusion and Restraint
NSW Health PD2015_007 Acute Severe Behavioural Disturbance

subtopic_detail: Post-sedation monitoring cadence and restraint release per NSW Health

stem:
A 22-year-old woman with a known diagnosis of borderline personality disorder presents to the mental health short-stay unit after deliberate self-harm by superficial cutting. On arrival she is tearful, increasingly loud, and shouting at the nurse that nobody listens. She has not yet been physically aggressive. She refuses to sit in the assessment room and is pacing the corridor near the exit.

Her file notes a previous adverse reaction to IM olanzapine (acute extrapyramidal symptoms) and a recent admission where physical restraint precipitated a flashback to a childhood assault. She is not currently intoxicated.

data_table.Examination: Tearful, loud, pacing, no current threats of harm to self or others beyond the existing cuts which have been dressed; alert and oriented.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first intervention?

options.text: Offer a low-stimulus space, a named clinician to sit with her, a clear acknowledgement of her distress, and the option of a PRN oral lorazepam if she wishes
options.rationale: Verbal de-escalation in a low-stimulus environment with a single consistent clinician is the first-line intervention for behavioural escalation, particularly where prior IM antipsychotic caused adverse effects and restraint precipitated trauma re-experiencing. Offering an oral PRN preserves agency and is consistent with trauma-informed care.

options.text: Administer IM olanzapine 10 mg now to prevent escalation
options.rationale: She has a documented adverse reaction to IM olanzapine; pre-emptive IM antipsychotic for non-aggressive distress is not justified. Would be wrong on both safety and indication grounds (Source: eTG; NSW Health).

options.text: Initiate four-point physical restraint and call security
options.rationale: Restraint is reserved for imminent serious risk; she is not currently aggressive, and her prior trauma history makes restraint particularly harmful. Would be wrong without imminent risk (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Discharge her with a follow-up appointment to reduce iatrogenic dependence on the unit
options.rationale: Discharge during active distress and immediately after self-harm is unsafe and inconsistent with BPD care guidance. Would be inappropriate at this point (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline).

options.text: Apply for involuntary admission under the Mental Health Act for treatment of personality disorder
options.rationale: Personality disorder alone is not grounds for involuntary admission under the NSW MHA; admission is justified only when there is comorbid mental illness or imminent risk not manageable otherwise. Would be wrong on legal grounds here (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

explanation.summary: The cornerstone of acute behavioural escalation management is verbal de-escalation in a low-stimulus space by a consistent clinician, with offered PRN oral medication preserving the patient's agency. Pharmacological and physical interventions are reserved for situations of imminent risk where de-escalation has failed. Trauma history shapes choice of intervention: physical restraint can re-traumatise patients with assault history.
key_points: Verbal de-escalation precedes pharmacological and physical intervention
Trauma-informed care is mandatory in BPD presentations
Personality disorder alone is not grounds for involuntary admission
Document previous adverse drug reactions and avoid
PRN oral lorazepam preserves agency
explanation.pearls: If de-escalation fails, the ladder is: oral PRN -> IM monotherapy -> seclusion as a last resort under formal authorisation.

subtopic_detail: Trauma-informed de-escalation for distressed BPD patient with documented adverse reactions

stem:
A 30-year-old man is brought into the resus bay at midnight by police. He was found shouting in traffic, was sweating profusely, and was 'fighting things in the air'. Vital signs on arrival: HR 138 sinus tachycardia, BP 178/104, RR 24, SpO2 99% on room air, temp 38.9 C. He is grossly diaphoretic, jaw is clenched, pupils 6 mm bilaterally and reactive.

He has multiple small abrasions on his arms. He weighs about 75 kg. He continues to shout that 'the lizards' are inside the resus bay. Two security staff are required to keep him on the trolley.

data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, dilated pupils, clenched jaw, hyperreflexia in lower limbs, no focal neurology, no obvious head injury, no track marks.
data_table.Investigations: VBG pending; finger-prick glucose 6.4 mmol/L. ECG sinus tachycardia, QTc 420 ms, no ischaemia.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological intervention?

options.text: IV diazepam 5 mg titrated to effect with airway support standby
options.rationale: Sympathomimetic toxidrome with hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia and hyperreflexia is best treated with IV benzodiazepines titrated to effect, which simultaneously reduces psychomotor agitation, sympathetic drive, hyperthermia, and seizure risk. Australian toxicology guidance positions benzodiazepines as the first-line agent. IV titration allows controlled escalation; airway readiness is essential given high cumulative doses are sometimes required.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre Methamphetamine toxidrome

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower the seizure threshold and offer no advantage over benzodiazepines for sympathetic-driven hyperthermia and hypertension. Would be a second-line addition only if benzodiazepines alone are inadequate and seizure risk is not high (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: IV beta-blocker (metoprolol 5 mg) for the hypertension
options.rationale: Beta-blockade in unopposed alpha stimulation (e.g. cocaine or methamphetamine toxidrome) risks worsening hypertension and is generally avoided. Would be wrong here (Source: Australian Resuscitation Council; toxicology consensus).

options.text: Active cooling alone and observe without sedation
options.rationale: Cooling is necessary but on its own does not address the agitation, sympathetic drive or seizure risk; benzodiazepines are required alongside cooling. Would be incomplete management (Source: Murray's Toxicology Handbook).

options.text: IV haloperidol 5 mg
options.rationale: IV haloperidol carries significant QT and torsades risk and is not first-line for sympathomimetic toxidrome; benzodiazepines target the underlying mechanism. Would be wrong here (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: Acute methamphetamine toxidrome presents with hyperadrenergic features (hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, mydriasis, diaphoresis, hyperreflexia) plus psychomotor agitation and often psychosis. Australian toxicology consensus treats this with IV benzodiazepines titrated to effect, active cooling, IV fluids and supportive care; antipsychotics are second-line because they lower seizure threshold and do not address the sympathetic drive. Beta-blocker monotherapy is avoided due to unopposed alpha stimulation.
key_points: Sympathomimetic toxidrome = benzodiazepines first-line
Avoid beta-blocker monotherapy
Active cooling alongside sedation
Antipsychotics are second-line; lower seizure threshold
Call NSW Poisons Information Centre 13 11 26 early
explanation.pearls: Hyperthermia >39 C is associated with rhabdomyolysis and DIC; aggressive cooling reduces mortality.

stem: A 27-year-old patient on a NSW acute mental health inpatient unit becomes acutely aggressive on the ward, throws furniture and threatens to kill the nurse-in-charge. He has been offered de-escalation, oral medication and a quiet area, all of which he has rejected. He is now placed in the seclusion room with a closed door. He weighs 90 kg and has had IM olanzapine 10 mg administered just before entering seclusion.

data_table.Investigations: Pre-event observations: HR 110, BP 140/85, RR 18, SpO2 98%, temp 37.0.

lead_in: Which observation and review schedule is the minimum required under NSW Health policy while he remains in seclusion?

options.text: Continuous direct visual observation, vital sign checks every 15 minutes, medical review within 1 hour, and senior psychiatrist review within 4 hours
options.rationale: NSW Health PD2012_035 requires continuous observation during seclusion, with vital signs at least every 15 minutes (and more frequently after parenteral medication), a medical review within an hour of seclusion commencement, and a senior psychiatrist review within 4 hours. The episode must be documented in real time with the indication, alternatives, and a debrief plan.
source_refs: NSW Health PD2012_035 Aggression, Seclusion and Restraint

options.text: Intermittent observation every 30 minutes, no requirement for medical review unless requested by the nursing team
options.rationale: Continuous direct observation is mandatory during seclusion; medical review is mandatory not optional. Would not meet NSW policy (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Continuous CCTV observation alone is sufficient without in-person checks
options.rationale: CCTV does not replace in-person observation; both are required. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Vital signs once at entry to seclusion, then again at release
options.rationale: Vital signs are required at least every 15 minutes, particularly after parenteral medication. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Seclusion can continue for a minimum of 4 hours regardless of clinical state to ensure full sedation
options.rationale: Seclusion duration is determined by clinical need, not a minimum time; release as soon as criteria are met. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

explanation.summary: Seclusion under NSW Health PD2012_035 requires continuous direct observation, vital signs at least every 15 minutes (more frequently if recent parenteral sedation), medical review within 1 hour, senior psychiatrist review within 4 hours, real-time documentation, and a debrief plan. Duration is determined by clinical need, not by a fixed minimum.
key_points: Continuous direct observation during seclusion
Vital signs at least every 15 minutes
Medical review within 1 hour
Senior psychiatrist review within 4 hours
Real-time documentation + debrief plan
explanation.pearls: Each seclusion event is reportable to the Chief Psychiatrist and reviewed for the use of least-restrictive practice.

subtopic_detail: Seclusion observation and review minimum standards

stem: A 55-year-old homeless man is brought to a regional NSW ED at 0200 with acute agitation. He smells strongly of alcohol, is unsteady on his feet, and is shouting incoherently. He has a known history of alcohol use disorder and previous severe alcohol withdrawal, and was last admitted 4 weeks ago. He weighs 70 kg. The team is considering IM sedation because he is intermittently swinging at staff. He has accepted no oral medication.

data_table.Examination: Smell of alcohol, ataxic gait, slurred speech, no obvious head injury, no focal neurology, GCS 14.
data_table.Investigations: Awaiting bloods; finger-prick glucose 5.0 mmol/L; ETOH on breath analyzer 0.18%.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: IM olanzapine 5 mg now
options.rationale: In an acutely intoxicated patient where alcohol is already a CNS depressant, IM olanzapine at a reduced dose (5 mg) is preferred over IM benzodiazepines, which would compound sedation and risk respiratory depression. NSW Health and eTG specifically caution against parenteral benzodiazepines in acutely intoxicated patients.

options.text: IM midazolam 10 mg
options.rationale: Compounded sedation with alcohol creates substantial respiratory depression risk; avoided in acute intoxication. Would be correct in a non-intoxicated agitated patient (Source: eTG).

options.text: IM diazepam 10 mg
options.rationale: Same compound-sedation risk as midazolam, plus diazepam has variable IM absorption and is not recommended IM. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Wait for blood alcohol to fall and avoid all medication
options.rationale: Active staff-directed aggression cannot be safely managed by waiting; choosing the safer agent is required. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2015_007).

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg plus IM lorazepam 2 mg
options.rationale: Combination therapy with a benzodiazepine in an intoxicated patient compounds respiratory depression; benzodiazepine should be avoided here. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: In acute behavioural disturbance with concurrent alcohol intoxication, parenteral benzodiazepines compound CNS depression and risk apnoea; IM olanzapine at a reduced dose is preferred. Note that this patient is also at high risk of alcohol withdrawal in the coming hours, and a separate withdrawal plan (thiamine, scheduled diazepam once alcohol levels are falling and the patient is safe) is required.
key_points: Avoid parenteral benzodiazepines in acute alcohol intoxication for behavioural sedation
IM olanzapine reduced-dose (5 mg) is the preferred choice in this scenario
Watch for and treat impending alcohol withdrawal separately
Always give parenteral thiamine before any glucose load
Reassess airway and SpO2 continuously
explanation.pearls: Two clinical phases coexist: (1) acute intoxication-related disturbance, treated with reduced-dose IM olanzapine; (2) impending withdrawal in 6-24 hours, treated with scheduled benzodiazepines.

subtopic_detail: Avoiding compounded sedation with parenteral benzodiazepines in alcohol intoxication

stem: A 38-year-old man with no past psychiatric history attends a Hunter New England LHD ED following a workplace incident. He is angry that he has been waiting 3 hours, has raised his voice once, but has not been physically aggressive. He paces the waiting area. The triage nurse asks for your approach to verbal de-escalation.

data_table.Examination: Raised voice, pacing, no clenched fists, no current threat.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first action?

options.text: Approach with one clinician, maintain a calm voice, acknowledge his frustration, offer a quiet space and an update on wait time
options.rationale: The Australasian College of Emergency Medicine and NSW Health guidance specify a single named clinician, calm tone, acknowledgement of feelings, and concrete information (wait time, plan) as the foundation of verbal de-escalation. This addresses the actual driver (information vacuum) before escalation.

options.text: Approach with three security staff to demonstrate the consequences of further escalation
options.rationale: A show of force tends to escalate; a single calm clinician is preferred at this stage with security in reserve. Would be wrong here (Source: ACEM).

options.text: Tell him he will be discharged without assessment if he raises his voice again
options.rationale: Threats and ultimatums escalate aggression and conflict with ED duty of care. Would be wrong (Source: ACEM).

options.text: Offer oral diazepam 10 mg as a first step
options.rationale: Pharmacological intervention is unnecessary at this stage; verbal de-escalation has not been tried. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Apply a Schedule 1 form to detain him under the Mental Health Act
options.rationale: He shows no features of mental illness or mental disorder; legal detention is not justified. Would be wrong (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

explanation.summary: Verbal de-escalation is the universal first-line response to behavioural escalation in the ED. The framework: one calm clinician, calm tone, respect personal space, acknowledge emotion, identify needs, offer concrete options. Threats, ultimatums and shows of force tend to escalate, not de-escalate.
key_points: One clinician, calm tone, named introduction
Acknowledge emotion before logistics
Offer concrete options (quiet space, update, food/drink)
Avoid threats and ultimatums
Reserve security as a visible but distant presence
explanation.pearls: The 10-domain model of verbal de-escalation (Richmond et al, 2012) is the most widely cited framework and underpins ACEM guidance.

stem: A 14-year-old boy with a diagnosis of autism spectrum disorder, intellectual disability and ADHD is brought to ED by his foster carer. He has been escalating over 48 hours, including hitting his foster sister and breaking furniture. On arrival to ED he is shouting, hitting himself in the head, and trying to bite staff. He weighs 50 kg. He takes risperidone 1 mg daily and methylphenidate XR 36 mg in the morning. Verbal de-escalation by his carer and a familiar paediatric nurse has not worked. He refuses oral medication.

data_table.Examination: Striking himself, attempting to bite, no acute medical distress.

lead_in: If pharmacological sedation becomes necessary, which is the single most appropriate first-line IM agent and dose?

options.text: IM olanzapine 5 mg
options.rationale: For an adolescent ~50 kg requiring IM sedation for severe behavioural disturbance, the Royal Children's Hospital Melbourne paediatric emergency guidance and NSW Health paediatric chemical restraint pathway position IM olanzapine 5 mg as a reasonable first-line option, with a lower respiratory depression risk than IM benzodiazepines and with an EPS profile compatible with his existing risperidone therapy.
source_refs: RCH Melbourne Acute behavioural disturbance in children and adolescents
NSW Health paediatric chemical restraint guidance

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg
options.rationale: 10 mg is the standard adult dose; in a 50 kg adolescent, droperidol if used would be at 2.5-5 mg, and is generally second-line behind olanzapine in this age group due to limited paediatric data. Would be wrong at this dose (Source: RCH).

options.text: IM diazepam 10 mg
options.rationale: IM diazepam has erratic absorption and is not recommended; midazolam is preferred if a benzodiazepine is used. Either way, antipsychotics are generally first-line in this scenario (Source: RCH).

options.text: IM ketamine 4 mg/kg
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for extreme situations where first-line agents have failed, under senior emergency physician direction. Would be wrong as first-line (Source: ACEM).

options.text: IM risperidone 2 mg
options.rationale: Standard oral risperidone is not formulated for acute IM administration in this setting; IM olanzapine is the established option. Would be wrong (Source: RCH).

explanation.summary: Paediatric severe behavioural disturbance follows the same ladder as adult (environment -> verbal de-escalation -> oral -> IM), but doses are weight-based and benzodiazepines have less favourable safety profiles than antipsychotics in non-substance-driven agitation. IM olanzapine 5 mg is a defensible first-line choice in a ~50 kg adolescent.
key_points: Apply the de-escalation ladder before IM sedation
IM olanzapine 5 mg is first-line in ~50 kg adolescents
Avoid IM diazepam; if a benzodiazepine is needed use IM midazolam
Continuous SpO2 + cardiac monitoring required
Involve paediatric mental health early
explanation.pearls: Family-centred care is essential: the carer's continued presence is usually de-escalating not escalating in autistic children.

subtopic_detail: Adolescent IM sedation choice in autism with ADHD

stem: A 78-year-old woman is admitted to a general medical ward with community-acquired pneumonia. Overnight she becomes acutely agitated, attempts to climb out of bed, removes her IV cannula, and shouts at imaginary visitors. She is normally living independently with mild memory impairment but no formal dementia diagnosis. Her vital signs: HR 102, BP 110/70, RR 24, SpO2 93% on 2 L/min nasal prongs, temp 38.4 C. She is non-cooperative with cognitive assessment. The night cover registrar is considering pharmacological sedation. Verbal de-escalation and reorientation have not settled her.

data_table.Examination: Agitated, picking at sheets, eyes darting, no focal neurology, mucous membranes dry, mildly tachypnoeic.
data_table.Investigations: VBG: pH 7.32, lactate 2.1 mmol/L. WCC 16, CRP 130 mg/L, Na 132 mmol/L, K 4.0, creatinine 110 micromol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Address reversible precipitants (oxygenation, analgesia, hydration, bladder, environment), and if pharmacological treatment is required for safety, give haloperidol 0.5 mg orally (or IM if oral declined)
options.rationale: Hyperactive delirium in the elderly is managed first by treating the underlying cause and addressing reversible factors (oxygen, hydration, pain, bladder/bowel, environment, sleep). If safety requires medication, low-dose haloperidol (0.5 mg) is the first-line agent in elderly delirium per the Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care delirium guidance and eTG; benzodiazepines are avoided as they worsen and prolong delirium except in alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal.

options.text: IM olanzapine 5 mg
options.rationale: Olanzapine is an option in delirium but at a lower starting dose (e.g. 2.5 mg) and is generally second-line in delirium behind low-dose haloperidol; 5 mg IM is excessive. Would be partially correct at 2.5 mg but excessive at 5 mg (Source: eTG).

options.text: IM midazolam 5 mg
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines worsen and prolong delirium except in alcohol/benzodiazepine withdrawal; avoid here. Would be wrong (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Four-point physical restraint to prevent further line removal
options.rationale: Physical restraint increases morbidity, mortality and delirium duration; reserved for failure of all alternatives and only for the shortest possible period. Would be wrong as next step (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Quetiapine 100 mg orally
options.rationale: Quetiapine has a role in some delirium scenarios but at much lower doses (e.g. 12.5-25 mg) and is not the first-line preferred agent in Australian guidance for hyperactive delirium. 100 mg is excessive. Would be wrong at this dose (Source: eTG).

explanation.summary: Hyperactive delirium in the elderly is first treated by addressing reversible causes; if pharmacological management becomes necessary for safety, low-dose haloperidol (0.5 mg PO or IM) is the Australian first-line. Benzodiazepines prolong and worsen delirium and are reserved for alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal. Physical restraint is a last resort.
key_points: First step: treat the cause (sepsis, pain, hypoxia, dehydration, bladder, sleep)
Pharmacological agent: low-dose haloperidol 0.5 mg as first-line
Avoid benzodiazepines (worsen and prolong delirium)
Avoid physical restraint where possible
Reorient, family presence, lighting cycle, mobilise
explanation.pearls: Doses 'too low' for adult psychosis are appropriate in delirium; titrate cautiously and reassess every 30-60 minutes.

subtopic_detail: Hyperactive delirium in elderly - first-line low-dose haloperidol after non-pharm

stem: A 26-year-old woman who is 32 weeks pregnant with her first child is brought to a Central Coast LHD ED with acute psychosis. She is shouting that the foetus has been replaced. She had escalating symptoms for 2 weeks. She has refused oral medication. She is becoming physically aggressive towards her partner. She has no medical history. Weight today 75 kg. Verbal de-escalation has failed.

data_table.Examination: Agitated, no obstetric concerns reported, foetal movements felt today; obstetric review en route.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate IM agent for pharmacological sedation?

options.text: IM olanzapine 10 mg
options.rationale: When IM antipsychotic sedation is required in late pregnancy, olanzapine is a reasonable choice with the strongest reproductive safety dataset among atypicals; obstetric, anaesthetic and psychiatric teams should be involved jointly. Foetal monitoring is required during and after. eTG and the MotherSafe NSW resource support olanzapine over haloperidol when foetal extrapyramidal effects need to be minimised at delivery if late in third trimester.

options.text: IM diazepam 10 mg
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines in late pregnancy risk neonatal sedation, respiratory depression and neonatal withdrawal; avoided when an alternative exists. Would be wrong (Source: MotherSafe).

options.text: IM haloperidol 10 mg
options.rationale: Haloperidol is acceptable in pregnancy but the dose proposed is high and EPS risk at delivery is higher than for olanzapine. Would be acceptable at lower dose (e.g. 2-5 mg) but olanzapine is generally preferred (Source: AMH).

options.text: IM droperidol 10 mg
options.rationale: Less reproductive safety data than olanzapine; not the preferred first choice in pregnancy. Would be wrong (Source: MotherSafe).

options.text: Wait for obstetric review before any pharmacological intervention
options.rationale: Active aggression in a 32-week pregnant woman creates immediate risk to her, the foetus and others; treatment cannot be deferred. Obstetric review can be parallel rather than sequential. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2015_007).

explanation.summary: Acute behavioural disturbance in pregnancy still follows the de-escalation ladder. When IM sedation is unavoidable, olanzapine is generally preferred in late pregnancy due to better reproductive safety data and a lower risk of neonatal extrapyramidal effects than typical antipsychotics. Joint obstetric, psychiatric and anaesthetic involvement is essential, with continuous foetal monitoring.
key_points: Do not defer treatment; harm of untreated severe psychosis is high
IM olanzapine preferred in late pregnancy when available
Benzodiazepines avoided in late pregnancy when alternative exists
Continuous foetal monitoring after sedation
MotherSafe NSW available 24 h for advice (02 9382 6539)
explanation.pearls: Postpartum, breastfeeding compatibility of acute medication choices should be re-reviewed; olanzapine and haloperidol have favourable LactMed profiles.

subtopic_detail: IM sedation choice in late pregnancy

stem: A 35-year-old man received IM droperidol 10 mg in a Hunter New England LHD ED for acute behavioural disturbance 45 minutes ago. The nurse calls you to review. His SpO2 has dropped from 98% on room air to 88%, RR 8, HR 110, BP 95/55. He is unrousable to voice but groans to sternal rub. Pupils 3 mm, equal and reactive. Capillary glucose 5.2 mmol/L.

data_table.Examination: GCS 7 (E1 V2 M4), shallow respirations, jaw soft, no obvious head injury.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia 110, QTc 470 ms (was 420 pre-sedation). VBG: pH 7.28, pCO2 60 mmHg, lactate 1.8 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Open the airway with a jaw thrust, apply high-flow oxygen, provide bag-valve-mask ventilation if RR remains low, and prepare for definitive airway support
options.rationale: He has post-sedation respiratory depression with hypercapnia and falling SpO2; the immediate priority is airway and ventilation per the ABC algorithm, with definitive airway support if assisted ventilation alone is insufficient. Flumazenil is not appropriate because the sedative is droperidol, not a benzodiazepine. Naloxone is not appropriate without opioid exposure.

options.text: IV flumazenil 200 micrograms
options.rationale: Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines; he received droperidol, not a benzodiazepine. Would be wrong here (Source: AMH).

options.text: IV naloxone 400 micrograms
options.rationale: Naloxone is for opioid toxicity; no opioid exposure is reported. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Administer IV fluids only and observe
options.rationale: Inadequate; the immediate threat is hypoventilation, not isolated hypotension. Would be wrong (Source: ARC).

options.text: Repeat IM droperidol 5 mg for ongoing sedation
options.rationale: Repeating the sedative will worsen respiratory depression; this is an over-sedation event. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2015_007).

explanation.summary: Post-sedation respiratory depression is the most common adverse event after parenteral sedation in acute behavioural disturbance. Management is ABC: open airway, supplemental oxygen, assisted ventilation, definitive airway if required. Reversal agents are agent-specific; flumazenil for benzodiazepine, naloxone for opioid; neither applies to droperidol.
key_points: Continuous SpO2 + cardiac monitoring is mandatory after IM sedation
Respiratory depression treated by airway opening + ventilation
Reversal agents are agent-specific
Flumazenil = benzodiazepines only
Naloxone = opioids only
explanation.pearls: Compounded sedation (combining IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour, or sedation in alcohol intoxication) is the highest-risk scenario; avoid by design.

stem: A 19-year-old man is brought to ED by friends from a music festival. He is severely agitated, sweating, with dilated pupils, hyperthermic at 39.5 C, hyponatraemic on a stat sodium of 122 mmol/L, and has had a witnessed brief generalised tonic-clonic seizure on arrival. Friends report he took 'three caps' of an unknown white powder and has been drinking water continuously to 'stay hydrated'. HR 130, BP 150/95, SpO2 99% room air.

data_table.Examination: Hyperthermic, diaphoretic, mydriasis, post-ictal, jaw clenched, GCS 13.
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (135-145), K 3.6 mmol/L, finger-prick glucose 5.6 mmol/L, urine ketones negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Restrict free water, give IV hypertonic saline (e.g. 150 mL of 3% saline bolus) to raise serum sodium and prevent further seizures; titrate IV benzodiazepines for ongoing agitation/seizure prevention; active cooling
options.rationale: MDMA-related severe hyponatraemia from excessive free-water intake plus SIADH-like effect causes cerebral oedema and seizures; correction with hypertonic saline under toxicology and ICU guidance is first-line. Concurrent benzodiazepines treat the sympathomimetic toxidrome and prevent further seizures. Active cooling addresses hyperthermia.
source_refs: NSW Poisons Information Centre MDMA toxicity

options.text: Aggressive IV normal saline 2 L bolus
options.rationale: Adding more isotonic fluid will worsen the dilutional hyponatraemia and cerebral oedema. Would be wrong (Source: NSW PIC).

options.text: IM olanzapine 10 mg as primary management
options.rationale: Antipsychotic monotherapy does not address hyponatraemia, hyperthermia, or seizure risk; benzodiazepines plus hypertonic saline are required. Would be wrong as primary management (Source: eTG).

options.text: IV furosemide 40 mg to enhance excretion
options.rationale: Loop diuretic in a hyponatraemic, possibly hypovolaemic patient will worsen the picture; not indicated. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Rapid correction of sodium to >140 mmol/L within 6 hours
options.rationale: Rapid over-correction risks osmotic demyelination; correction is by small hypertonic saline boluses to control symptoms, not to normalise sodium rapidly. Would be wrong (Source: eTG; NSW PIC).

explanation.summary: MDMA toxicity classically combines sympathomimetic toxidrome (hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, mydriasis), serotonin-related features (hyperreflexia, clonus) and dilutional hyponatraemia from excess free-water intake plus an SIADH-like mechanism. Severe hyponatraemia with cerebral oedema and seizures requires hypertonic saline, not normal saline. Benzodiazepines are central to managing the toxidrome.
key_points: MDMA + free water = severe dilutional hyponatraemia
Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: hypertonic saline boluses
Benzodiazepines for sympathomimetic features and seizure prophylaxis
Active cooling for hyperthermia
Call NSW PIC 13 11 26 for advice
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemia at a festival in a young person should prompt early consideration of MDMA, especially with the combination of hyperthermia and seizures.

stem: A 26-year-old Aboriginal man from a remote community attends a regional NSW ED with acute distress and behavioural escalation after the unexpected death of a family member earlier that day. He is pacing the corridor, intermittently weeping, then yelling. He is not currently aggressive towards staff but has thrown a chair. He has no past mental health diagnosis. He has a strong distrust of clinical staff. His older cousin and an Aboriginal Health Worker who knows him well are present in the waiting room. He has refused oral medication.

data_table.Examination: Distressed, tearful, intermittently shouting, no overt threat to staff, no signs of substance use.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate first action?

options.text: Invite the Aboriginal Health Worker and cousin in to provide a culturally safe space, low stimulus environment, and time for grief; involve the local Aboriginal Mental Health team early
options.rationale: Acute grief in a culturally significant context is not necessarily a psychiatric emergency and is best supported by culturally safe care including involvement of the Aboriginal Health Worker, family, and the local Aboriginal Mental Health team. NSW Health and RANZCP cultural safety guidance prioritise non-pharmacological, culturally-informed responses where possible. The NSW Aboriginal Health Plan and the IAHA framework both emphasise this approach.
source_refs: RANZCP Position Statement: Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander mental health

options.text: Administer IM olanzapine 10 mg
options.rationale: He is distressed and grieving, not psychotic or aggressive towards staff. Pharmacological sedation as a first move would be culturally unsafe and clinically inappropriate. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2015_007).

options.text: Apply Schedule 1 of the Mental Health Act to detain him
options.rationale: Grief is not a mental illness and does not meet the threshold for involuntary detention. Would be wrong (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

options.text: Discharge home with a sedative prescription
options.rationale: Premature discharge without cultural and clinical support is unsafe; benzodiazepine prescription in unsupported acute grief is not best practice. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Refuse any family or AHW involvement to maintain a clinical assessment environment
options.rationale: Excluding family and AHW from a grieving Aboriginal man's care is culturally unsafe and likely to escalate distress. Would be wrong (Source: RANZCP).

explanation.summary: Distress and behavioural escalation in the context of acute culturally significant loss in an Aboriginal patient is first managed with culturally safe care: AHW and family involvement, low-stimulus environment, and engagement of the local Aboriginal Mental Health team. Pharmacological and legal interventions are not the first move and should be considered only if escalation creates genuine safety risk.
key_points: Cultural safety is part of clinical safety, not separate from it
AHW involvement should be routine in Aboriginal patient presentations
Grief is not a mental illness
Avoid pharmacological + legal interventions as a first response
Engage Aboriginal Mental Health Worker / team early
explanation.pearls: Sorry Business has specific cultural meaning; ask if it is appropriate to discuss the deceased's name and observe local protocols.

sources.label: RANZCP Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander mental health

subtopic_detail: Culturally safe response to acute grief in Aboriginal patient

stem: A 50-year-old man with a long history of schizophrenia presents to a Central Coast LHD ED in acute relapse: paranoid, irritable, refusing to engage. He is well known to the local community team. Today he is calm enough to talk but refuses any medication, stating 'it's all poison'. He believes the staff are trying to murder him. He is not currently aggressive but is at risk of leaving the unit. His consultant is on the way. Capacity is being assessed.

data_table.Examination: Alert, calm, paranoid, no current aggression, no acute medical issue.

lead_in: Which of the following best describes the relationship between capacity, the NSW Mental Health Act 2007, and the decision to administer treatment without consent?

options.text: Lack of capacity is not a prerequisite for involuntary treatment under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007; treatment may be given to a detained patient if the criteria of the Act are met, irrespective of capacity status
options.rationale: Under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007, a person detained as a mentally ill or mentally disordered person may receive treatment in accordance with the Act regardless of whether they retain decision-making capacity for that specific treatment. Capacity assessment remains clinically important and informs the Tribunal's review, but is not a statutory prerequisite for treatment under this Act (unlike the Mental Capacity model used in other jurisdictions).

options.text: Capacity assessment must demonstrate lack of capacity before any involuntary treatment can be given under the NSW Mental Health Act 2007
options.rationale: This misstates the NSW Act; capacity is not a statutory prerequisite (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

options.text: Consent is required from the next of kin before treatment of a detained mentally ill person
options.rationale: Next-of-kin substitute consent is not the legal mechanism for treatment under the NSW MHA. Would be wrong (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

options.text: ECT can be given to any detained patient without further authorisation
options.rationale: ECT requires specific consent, or, where the patient is unable to consent, authorisation by the Mental Health Review Tribunal. Would be wrong (Source: NSW MHA 2007 Part 2 Division 1).

options.text: A patient detained under the NSW MHA must give written consent to each medication dose
options.rationale: Written consent to each dose is not required; the legal authority to treat lies in the Act once the patient is detained. Would be wrong (Source: NSW MHA 2007).

explanation.summary: The NSW Mental Health Act 2007 authorises treatment of detained patients without statutorily requiring lack of capacity, though capacity assessment remains best practice. This differs from other Australian states (e.g. Victoria, Queensland) which use capacity-based criteria for treatment without consent. Special authorisations apply for specific treatments such as ECT and psychosurgery.
key_points: Capacity is not a statutory prerequisite for involuntary treatment under NSW MHA
ECT requires specific authorisation (consent or MHRT)
Substitute consent (next-of-kin) is not the mechanism in NSW MHA
Clinical capacity assessment remains important and is documented
Differs from Victoria's capacity-based model
explanation.pearls: Capacity is decision- and time-specific; document each assessment in the clinical record.

subtopic_detail: Capacity vs NSW MHA 2007 treatment authority

stem: A 24-year-old woman in a Central Coast LHD ED receives IM olanzapine 10 mg for severe behavioural disturbance. Twenty minutes later, the nurse calls you: she is profoundly hypotensive (BP 70/40), bradycardic (HR 42), GCS 9, with no urticaria, no wheeze, and warm dry skin. She remains afebrile. She has had no other medication in the last 12 hours per her partner. The team is asking whether this is anaphylaxis.

data_table.Examination: Pale, peripheries warm, no rash, lungs clear, abdomen soft, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus bradycardia at 42, no ischaemia, QTc 430 ms. VBG: pH 7.34, lactate 2.5 mmol/L.

lead_in: Which is the single most likely mechanism and most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Alpha-1 blockade and vasovagal-type bradycardia from IM olanzapine; manage with IV fluids, atropine 600 micrograms IV if symptomatic bradycardia, and supportive care; reserve adrenaline only if anaphylaxis features develop
options.rationale: Olanzapine has notable alpha-1 antagonism producing orthostatic hypotension and, rarely, profound IM-related hypotensive bradycardia (a recognised post-marketing adverse event). Anaphylaxis would feature urticaria, wheeze, angio-oedema and tachycardia, not bradycardia with warm dry skin. Management is supportive: IV fluids, atropine for symptomatic bradycardia, vasopressor support if needed. Adrenaline reserved for true anaphylaxis features.
source_refs: TGA Olanzapine Product Information adverse reactions

options.text: IM adrenaline 0.5 mg as for anaphylaxis
options.rationale: Adrenaline in alpha-1-mediated hypotension without true anaphylaxis is not the first-line response; absence of urticaria, wheeze and tachycardia argues against anaphylaxis. Would be reserved for true anaphylaxis features (Source: ASCIA guidelines).

options.text: Treat as neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS features hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, autonomic instability with raised CK, evolving over 24-72 hours; this is not the picture. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

options.text: Treat as serotonin syndrome
options.rationale: Serotonin syndrome requires a serotonergic agent and features clonus, hyperreflexia, hyperthermia; none apply. Would be wrong (Source: eTG).

options.text: Repeat IM olanzapine 5 mg for ongoing agitation
options.rationale: Repeating the offending agent during a hypotensive bradycardic adverse reaction would worsen the picture. Would be wrong (Source: AMH).

explanation.summary: IM olanzapine can rarely produce profound orthostatic hypotension with reflex or vagally-mediated bradycardia, especially in benzodiazepine co-administration. The picture is mechanistically distinct from anaphylaxis (no urticaria, no wheeze, bradycardia rather than tachycardia, warm dry skin). Management is supportive with IV fluids, atropine if symptomatic bradycardia, and vasopressor support if shock persists. The post-marketing adverse profile of IM olanzapine includes 28 reported cases of fatal hypotension by 2010, often with concurrent benzodiazepine.
key_points: Olanzapine alpha-1 antagonism explains hypotension
Bradycardia + warm dry skin argues against anaphylaxis
IV fluids + atropine first; adrenaline only for true anaphylaxis
Avoid further olanzapine in this admission
Document adverse reaction in the medical record and patient alert system
explanation.pearls: This is the mechanism behind the NSW Health rule against IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine within 1 hour.

subtopic_detail: Recognising IM olanzapine alpha-1-mediated hypotensive bradycardia vs anaphylaxis

stem: Following a successful de-escalation in which a patient was placed in brief seclusion and given IM sedation, the ED team is preparing for the post-event debrief.
lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate component of post-event practice mandated by NSW Health policy?

options.text: A patient debrief once the patient is awake and able to engage, and a staff debrief to review what worked and what did not
options.rationale: NSW Health PD2012_035 mandates both patient and staff debriefs after restrictive practices. Patient debrief offers an opportunity to repair therapeutic alliance, identify triggers, and contribute to safety planning. Staff debrief identifies system, team and individual learning.
source_refs: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care

options.text: No debrief is required if the event was resolved without injury
options.rationale: Debriefs are required for all restrictive practice events under NSW policy; absence of injury does not waive this. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Only the consultant attends the debrief
options.rationale: Debriefs include the full clinical team and where appropriate the patient; not consultant-only. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

options.text: Debriefs occur 2 weeks after the event for emotional distance
options.rationale: Hot debrief shortly after the event is preferred for safety and learning, with cold debrief at follow-up. Would be wrong (Source: ACSQHC).

options.text: Debriefs are only for restraint events involving security
options.rationale: All restrictive practice events trigger debrief obligations. Would be wrong (Source: NSW Health PD2012_035).

explanation.summary: Post-event debrief, including both staff and patient elements, is a mandatory component of restrictive practice under NSW Health policy. The patient debrief supports therapeutic repair and safety planning; the staff debrief drives system learning and reduces future restrictive practice.
key_points: Both patient and staff debriefs required after restrictive practice
Patient debrief supports therapeutic alliance + safety planning
Staff debrief is hot + cold
Document outcomes
All restrictive practice events trigger reporting and review
explanation.pearls: Restrictive practice reduction is a national safety priority; trends should be monitored at unit level.

subtopic_detail: Mandatory patient and staff debrief after restrictive practice
